have the tool pre-loaded with Oracle documentation
I do not have to search through Oracle's website manually
Acceptance Criteria: 1. At least 10 Oracle reference documents exist in data/oracle_docs/. 2. Each document covers a different Oracle module or OIC concept. 3. Documents are readable and clearly sourced.

have the document library update its index only when files change
the tool does not waste time re-reading the same documents on every run
Acceptance Criteria: 1. On first run the index is built and saved to disk. 2. On second run with no file changes the index loads from disk in under 2 seconds. 3. If a document is added or edited the index rebuilds automatically.

type my integration requirement in plain English
I do not need to know any technical format or Oracle terminology upfront
Acceptance Criteria: 1. A sentence like "Sync Workday hires to Oracle HCM nightly" is accepted as valid input. 2. The tool identifies the source system, target system, and integration type from the sentence. 3. No error is thrown for reasonable plain-English variations.

have the tool automatically identify the correct integration pattern
I know whether to build a scheduled sync or an event-driven flow before any design work begins
Acceptance Criteria: 1. Scheduled keywords ("every night", "daily", "at 2am") map to the Scheduled pattern. 2. Event keywords ("when an order is created", "on approval") map to the Event-Driven pattern. 3. The identified pattern appears in the generated spec.

have the tool retry if its first attempt to read my requirement fails
a single formatting issue in the AI response does not crash the whole tool
Acceptance Criteria: 1. If the first AI response is invalid the tool tries a second time automatically. 2. The second attempt includes the error message from the first so the AI can self-correct. 3. After two failed attempts a clear error message is shown — not a stack trace.

see the exact API endpoints the integration will call
I have a concrete starting point instead of a blank page
Acceptance Criteria: 1. The spec includes a source endpoint (e.g. Workday REST path) and a target endpoint (e.g. Oracle HCM REST path). 2. Each endpoint includes the authentication method. 3. A sample request and response payload is shown for both systems.

see a field-by-field mapping table in the generated spec
I know exactly which field in System A becomes which field in System B and how to convert it
Acceptance Criteria: 1. The mapping table lists source field, target field, transformation rule, and whether the field is required. 2. At least the most important fields are covered. 3. Transformation rules use real examples (e.g. date format conversion, prefix, lookup).

see error handling steps in every generated spec
I never forget to design what happens when a record fails
Acceptance Criteria: 1. The spec includes at least one per-record error handling step. 2. The spec includes a catch-all handler for integration-level failures. 3. If a notification (Slack, email) was mentioned in the requirement it appears in the error handling section.

have the tool flag its own assumptions before I read the spec
I can focus my review on the decisions that matter rather than hunting for hidden guesses
Acceptance Criteria: 1. The Assumptions section lists at least 3 items the AI inferred but did not confirm. 2. The Open Questions section lists things the requirement did not specify. 3. Both sections are populated by a second independent AI pass not the same one that wrote the spec.

be able to skip the critic step when I just want a quick draft
I can get a result faster during early exploration without waiting for two AI calls
Acceptance Criteria: 1. Running the tool with --no-critic skips the second AI call. 2. The output still contains all spec sections — assumptions and open questions may just be shorter. 3. The Streamlit UI has a checkbox to toggle this.

download the generated spec as a Markdown file
I can share it with my team or paste it into Confluence without reformatting
Acceptance Criteria: 1. A --output flag on the CLI saves the spec to a named .md file. 2. The Streamlit UI has a Download as Markdown button. 3. The downloaded file opens correctly in any Markdown viewer.

non-technical stakeholder being shown the design
see a simple diagram of the integration flow
I can understand how the systems connect without reading the technical detail
Acceptance Criteria: 1. Every generated spec includes a Mermaid sequence diagram. 2. The diagram shows the correct actors based on the integration type (scheduled shows a scheduler trigger; event-driven shows a webhook). 3. The diagram renders in the Streamlit UI and in GitHub Markdown preview.

run the tool with a single command and get the spec printed to the screen
I can use it in a script or pipeline without opening a browser
Acceptance Criteria: 1. python -m copilot "<requirement>" works with no additional setup after installation. 2. Output is clean Markdown printed to stdout. 3. Flags like --k --model and --verbose work as documented.

use a simple browser interface to generate and show a spec
I can demonstrate the tool to a client without anyone needing to use the terminal
Acceptance Criteria: 1. streamlit run app.py opens a working UI in the browser. 2. The UI has a text box for the requirement and shows the generated spec on the same screen. 3. The UI shows a loading indicator while generating so it does not appear frozen.

have automated tests for the core logic
I can change the code confidently knowing I will be told immediately if something breaks
Acceptance Criteria: 1. Tests exist for the parser retry logic. 2. Tests exist for the retrieval query construction. 3. Tests exist for all Pydantic schema validations. 4. All tests pass with pytest and require no API key or live internet connection.
